/home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/anthropic-claude-sonnet-4.6/aur-sleuth-297x5ckb/PKGBUILD: [SKIPPED] Skipping binary file: PKGBUILD

data.reasoning: Both models skipped the only reviewed file (PKGBUILD) because it was misclassified as a 'binary file'. No actual content was analyzed by either model — zero tokens consumed, zero files reviewed. This is a tooling failure, not a security finding. No verdict on package safety can be reached because the PKGBUILD, the most critical file determining what executes during makepkg, was never examined.
data.disagreement_analysis: There is no disagreement. Both models reached identical 'skipped' results for the identical reason: the PKGBUILD was flagged as a binary file by the pre-filter and never passed to the model for review.
coverage_issues: PKGBUILD was never reviewed — misclassified as a binary file by the file-type detection logic
Zero files were actually analyzed across both reports; the audit provides no security coverage whatsoever
No .install hooks, source files, or supplementary scripts were enumerated or reviewed (though for a headers package these are typically minimal)
false_negatives: Entire PKGBUILD content unexamined — any build/package function behavior, network fetches, or sources are completely unknown
Any .install script (common for kernel/header packages to run depmod or similar) was not enumerated or reviewed
re_audit_focus: PKGBUILD (force text-mode review; fix binary misclassification)
sources array and validpgpkeys / sha256sums for upstream tarball integrity
learnings: The binary-file detection heuristic incorrectly flagged a plain-text PKGBUILD as binary, blocking all review. PKGBUILD should be whitelisted/forced to text-mode parsing since it is always a shell script.
When the initial PKGBUILD safety check fails due to file-type misclassification (not actual content concern), the tool should retry with explicit text decoding rather than aborting the whole audit.
A 'skipped' result with 0 files reviewed and 0 tokens should never be treated as a substantive verdict — surface it distinctly as a tooling error requiring re-run, not a package-level conclusion.

Consider detecting unusual byte sequences (e.g., null bytes, encoding artifacts, trailing binary blobs appended to a script) — if the PKGBUILD genuinely contained binary data that would itself be a notable finding worth escalating rather than silently skipping.

data.reasoning: Both models skipped the only critical file (PKGBUILD) due to erroneous binary file classification. Without PKGBUILD review, package safety cannot be determined.
coverage_issues: Critical gap: PKGBUILD (build/install instructions) was skipped by both models. No .install hooks or scripts were reviewed.
All models reviewed 0 files despite PKGBUILD being essential for AUR safety assessment.
false_negatives: Potential risks in PKGBUILD (malicious commands, unsafe downloads) were completely missed due to skipped review
learnings: Fix file type detection: PKGBUILD is always text. Implement MIME-type verification before skipping 'binary' files.
Prioritize critical files: PKGBUILD must be reviewed first; abort audit if unreadable.
Add validation: Flag when essential files (PKGBUILD/.install) are skipped as errors.
Improve error handling: Retry with hexdump fallback when text file misclassification occurs.
